properties.name: Partington Cove
properties.hook: An unmarked trail off a blind pullout that drops through a rock tunnel to a cove most people driving past never know is there.

properties.name: Pescadero
properties.hook: A two-street farm town in the fog-and-redwood country where people drive an hour for artichoke soup and warm garlic bread.

properties.name: The Petrified Forest
properties.hook: Giant redwoods turned entirely to stone by a volcanic eruption three million years ago, lying where they fell, near Calistoga.

properties.name: The Pez Museum
properties.hook: A little Burlingame storefront holding the world's largest collection of Pez dispensers, plus a museum of toys that got banned.

properties.name: Pfeiffer Beach
properties.hook: Purple sand and an offshore rock with a keyhole the winter sun fires straight through, reached by a road with no sign.

properties.name: Pfeiffer Falls & Valley View
properties.hook: A short redwood loop to a 60-foot fall, the easy leg-stretch inside the park most people only drive through to reach Pfeiffer Beach.

properties.name: Picchetti Ranch & Winery
properties.hook: One of the oldest surviving wineries in California still stands on the Monte Bello ridge above Cupertino — an 1870s Italian homestead where peacocks strut the grounds and you can taste wine on the porch of a building older than the state's wine industry.

properties.name: Piedra Blanca
properties.hook: Round a bend in the Sespe backcountry above Ojai and a cluster of enormous pale sandstone domes rises out of the chaparral, smooth and strange and made for climbing on.

properties.name: Piedras Blancas Elephant Seal Rookery
properties.hook: Thousands of elephant seals sprawled on a beach a few feet below a boardwalk, for free, any day of the year.

properties.name: Pigeon Point Lighthouse
properties.hook: One of the tallest lighthouses on the West Coast, with a hostel in the keepers' houses and a hot tub bolted to the edge of the cliff.

properties.name: Pinnacles National Park
properties.hook: The Bay Area's own national park is the one nobody visits: half of an ancient volcano that drifted 195 miles up the San Andreas Fault, full of caves you scramble through and condors overhead.

properties.name: Pioneertown & Pappy and Harriet's
properties.hook: In 1946 a group of Western movie stars built a fake 1880s town in the high desert to film in and live in, and it's still standing, still a real town, with a roadhouse out back that books serious music.

properties.name: Papua New Guinea Sculpture Garden
properties.hook: A quiet grove near Stanford full of towering carved totems and a dugout canoe, made by New Guinea artists flown in to carve the wood on site.

properties.name: Point Arena & the Stornetta Lands
properties.hook: The tallest lighthouse on the Pacific coast stands where the San Andreas Fault runs out to sea — beside miles of public bluffs pocked with sinkholes, sea arches, and blowholes you wander with no fences and no crowds.

properties.name: Point Bonita Lighthouse
properties.hook: The only lighthouse in America you reach by crossing a suspension bridge — out a hand-carved rock tunnel and over a miniature Golden Gate to a light clinging to the last crag of the Marin Headlands, where the Bay meets the ocean.

properties.name: Point Dume
properties.hook: A rocky headland juts out of the Malibu coast with a cove and tide pools below and, in winter, gray whales passing close enough to watch from the bluff.

properties.name: Point Fermin Lighthouse
properties.hook: A gingerbread Victorian lighthouse from 1874 stands on the San Pedro bluffs, the twin of a lighthouse the atlas already loves up in San Francisco Bay.

properties.name: Point Isabel
properties.hook: One of the largest off-leash dog parks in the country — 23 acres of Richmond bayshore where a half-million dog visits a year happen under nothing but voice control, all with the Golden Gate Bridge as the backdrop.

properties.name: Point Lobos State Natural Reserve
properties.hook: The one Big Sur stop worth setting an alarm for, and the reason to be at a gate at 8am on vacation.

properties.name: Big Sycamore Canyon & La Jolla Valley (Point Mugu)
properties.hook: Where the Santa Monica Mountains meet the sea at the Ventura county line, a canyon of old sycamores runs inland to one of the last native grasslands in Southern California, and in autumn the monarchs come.

properties.name: Point Reyes Lighthouse & Chimney Rock
properties.hook: The windiest, foggiest point on the West Coast: 308 steps down to a lighthouse on the cliff, and in winter a beach heaving with elephant seals over the ridge.

properties.name: Point Sur Lighthouse
properties.hook: A working lighthouse stranded on a 361-foot volcanic rock, and the only one on the coast you can walk through, on a docent's schedule.

properties.name: Poly Canyon (the Architecture Graveyard)
properties.hook: A canyon behind Cal Poly holds sixty years of student-built experimental structures — a shell house, a bridge house, geodesic domes — slowly weathering where their builders left them.

properties.name: Port Costa
properties.hook: A dead-end former grain port on the Carquinez Strait that time forgot: one street, a Victorian roadhouse, and a cavernous warehouse bar with a stuffed polar bear.

properties.name: Portola Redwoods State Park
properties.hook: A deep, quiet redwood canyon at the far side of the Skyline hills that takes real effort to reach, so almost nobody bothers.

properties.name: Preston Castle
properties.hook: A 120-room Romanesque castle rises over the Gold Country town of Ione — built in 1894 not as a mansion but as a reform school, and abandoned with a reputation dark enough that ghost-hunting TV keeps coming back.

properties.name: Museum of International Propaganda
properties.hook: A small, free San Rafael museum of a century's worth of state propaganda, one couple's unsettling collection of posters, cartoons, and personality-cult kitsch.

properties.name: Pulgas Water Temple
properties.hook: A Roman-style temple hidden behind a gate on a country road, built in 1934 to celebrate water arriving from 160 miles away.

properties.name: Purisima Creek Redwoods
properties.hook: A deep redwood canyon on the coast side of Skyline that most people blow past on the way to the beach, with trails from flat creek walks to a lung-busting climb.

properties.name: The Rae Lakes Loop
properties.hook: A forty-mile loop out of Kings Canyon climbs to a chain of alpine lakes under a spire called the Painted Lady, over a twelve-thousand-foot pass, on some of the best trail in the Sierra.

properties.name: Ragged Point & Black Swift Falls
properties.hook: The cliff where Big Sur begins or ends, with a brutal little trail down to a black-sand beach and a 300-foot waterfall you can't see from the top.

properties.name: Rancho Corral de Tierra
properties.hook: Thousands of acres of wild coastal hills rising straight up behind the Highway 1 towns, a big empty backcountry hiding in plain sight above Montara.

properties.name: Rancho San Antonio & Deer Hollow Farm
properties.hook: The most-loved trail network on the Peninsula hides a 160-year-old working homestead in its folds — a real farm with pigs, goats, and a cow, a mile's flat walk from a Cupertino parking lot, at the foot of 25 miles of hills.

properties.name: Reinhardt Redwood Regional Park
properties.hook: Ships entering the Golden Gate once navigated by redwoods on this Oakland ridge so huge they were visible 16 miles out at sea — all cut by 1860, and the forest that grew back is now the East Bay's secret cathedral.

properties.name: Rincon Point
properties.hook: The most famous wave in California peels for hundreds of yards around a point on the Santa Barbara-Ventura line, and on a good winter swell you can ride from one county into the next.

properties.name: Ring Mountain
properties.hook: A Tiburon ridge with 2,400-year-old Native carvings pecked into the boulders, a wildflower found nowhere else on Earth, and a full sweep of the bay.

properties.name: Roaring Camp Railroads
properties.hook: Century-old steam locomotives haul you up some of the steepest railroad grades in North America, straight through old-growth redwoods — on engines dating to 1890, among the oldest authentically preserved narrow-gauge steam still running regular passenger service in America.

properties.name: Rodin Sculpture Garden (Cantor)
properties.hook: Rodin's Gates of Hell, and the largest collection of his bronzes outside Paris, sitting free and outdoors on the Stanford campus.

properties.name: Rose Valley Falls
properties.hook: A three-hundred-foot waterfall drops in two tiers over mossy rock at the end of a short, easy trail in the mountains above Ojai.

properties.name: Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum
properties.hook: The largest collection of Egyptian artifacts on the US West Coast — real mummies included — built and run by a mystical order in a San Jose neighborhood, in a campus styled after an Egyptian temple complex.

properties.name: Rosie the Riveter NHP (Richmond)
properties.hook: Richmond built 747 ships in four years — a Liberty ship in under five days at the record — and the national park telling that story sits in the very shipyards, with one of the last Victory ships still afloat at the dock.

properties.name: Russian Ridge & Borel Hill
properties.hook: The highest named point in San Mateo County sits on an open grass ridge above Skyline — and in April it detonates into one of the best wildflower displays on the Peninsula, poppies and lupine to the horizon, with the ocean on one side and the whole bay on the other.

properties.name: Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge
properties.hook: On winter afternoons a cloud of snow geese lifts off a Central Valley marsh all at once, loud enough to feel, and you can watch it from a levee road.

properties.name: Safari West
properties.hook: Four hundred acres of Sonoma hills run as an African savanna — giraffes, rhinos, cheetahs — with luxury safari tents imported from Botswana, and a fire story that tells you everything about the place.

properties.name: The Salk Institute
properties.hook: Louis Kahn's most famous building is a bare travertine courtyard split by a single thin line of water that runs straight out to the Pacific.

properties.name: Salmon Creek Falls
properties.hook: A 120-foot waterfall a five-minute walk from a highway pullout at the far south end, where almost nobody bothers to stop.

properties.name: Salt Point State Park
properties.hook: Six thousand acres of Sonoma coast where the sandstone bluffs are eaten into honeycomb lacework, one of California's first underwater parks lies just offshore, and the ridgetop forest is a garden of full-grown trees stunted to knee height.

properties.name: The Salton Sea & Bombay Beach
properties.hook: California's largest lake was an accident, a river breach that flooded the desert in 1905, and its dead resort towns are now the eeriest landscape in the state.

properties.name: Salvation Mountain
properties.hook: One man spent thirty years painting a hill in the desert, with half a million gallons of paint, to say as big as he could that God is love.

properties.name: The Samoa Cookhouse
properties.hook: The last lumber-camp cookhouse in the West still feeds you the way it fed loggers in 1893: family-style at long tables, in bowls you pass around, until nobody leaves hungry.

properties.name: Sam's Castle
properties.hook: An actual stone castle on a Pacifica hillside, built after 1906 by a man who wanted to be earthquake-proof, with a wild century behind it.

properties.name: San Bruno Mountain
properties.hook: A wild mountain marooned in the middle of the urban peninsula, with rare butterflies and a summit view from the ocean to the bay to the Farallones.

properties.name: San Carpóforo Beach
properties.hook: The wide, driftwood-strewn beach at the very bottom of Big Sur that almost nobody stops for, because there's no sign telling them to.

properties.name: San Gorgonio Mountain (Old Greyback)
properties.hook: The highest mountain in Southern California isn't in the Sierra. It's Old Greyback, an 11,503-foot dome above the Inland Empire, high enough that its summit clears the treeline entirely.

properties.name: San Gregorio General Store
properties.hook: An 1889 general store that's really a saloon with live music, stiff Bloody Marys, and shelves of books, boots, and cast iron.

properties.name: Mount San Jacinto Peak & Cactus to Clouds
properties.hook: From the desert floor in Palm Springs, one of the steepest big climbs in the country rises more than ten thousand feet to an alpine summit, and John Muir thought the view from the top the finest on Earth.

properties.name: San Jose Flea Market
properties.hook: "La Pulga" — one of the largest open-air markets in America since 1960 — is eight lanes of produce rows, taco smoke, quinceañera dresses, and live banda that most of the Bay Area north of it has never visited.

properties.name: San Pedro Valley & Brooks Falls
properties.hook: Twenty-five minutes from San Francisco, a three-tier waterfall drops 175 feet down a Pacifica canyon each winter — and most of the city has never heard of it.

properties.name: Sand Dollar Beach & Jade Cove
properties.hook: The one place on 90 miles of cliff coast where you can walk a long, open beach, and dig real jade out of the surf next door.

properties.name: Santa Barbara County Courthouse
properties.hook: A working county courthouse is one of the most beautiful public buildings in the country, and you can ride an elevator up its clock tower for a free view over the whole city to the sea.

properties.name: Santa Paula
properties.hook: A citrus town that time mostly left alone still calls itself the Citrus Capital of the World, its main street lined with murals and a century-old oil headquarters turned museum.

properties.name: The Sausalito Houseboats
properties.hook: A floating neighborhood of wildly individual houseboats on the Sausalito waterfront, the mellow, arty remnant of a 1960s bohemia.

properties.name: Sawyer Camp Trail & Crystal Springs
properties.hook: A paved car-free trail running straight down the San Andreas Fault, along two reservoirs, past a laurel tree older than California.

properties.name: Charles M. Schulz Museum
properties.hook: Charles Schulz drew Peanuts in Santa Rosa for thirty years — and the museum across from his beloved ice rink holds the drawn-by-hand evidence of the most widely read comic strip in history.

properties.name: Sea Ranch Chapel
properties.hook: A tiny, wave-shaped chapel on the Sonoma coast that looks like a seashell grew out of the meadow, open to anyone, of any faith or none.

properties.name: Self-Realization Fellowship Lake Shrine
properties.hook: A spring-fed lake ringed by gardens and a windmill chapel hides off Sunset Boulevard, with a portion of Gandhi's ashes enshrined at the water's edge, open to anyone for free.

properties.name: The Junípero Serra Statue
properties.hook: A 26-foot concrete Junípero Serra kneeling by the freeway and pointing west, that locals swear looks sculpted from mashed potatoes.

properties.name: Sespe Wilderness & the Condor Sanctuary
properties.hook: The largest flying land bird in North America came back from twenty-two individuals, and this wild river canyon northeast of Fillmore is where it was set free again.

properties.name: The Seward Street Slides
properties.hook: Two-story concrete slides built into one of the city's steepest hills, designed by a fourteen-year-old, ridden on a scrap of cardboard.

properties.name: Sherman Library & Gardens
properties.hook: A full city block in Corona del Mar hides a lush botanical garden and research library, built by one man who kept buying up the lots next door.

properties.name: Shields Date Garden
properties.hook: A 1924 date farm in Indio serves a date milkshake that tastes like caramel and, in its own little theater, screens a film called "The Romance and Sex Life of the Date."

properties.name: Sibley Volcanic Preserve
properties.hook: A ten-million-year-old volcano lies in the Oakland hills — tipped on its side by the Hayward Fault so you can walk straight into its guts — and someone has laid stone labyrinths in the old quarry pits at the bottom.

properties.name: Sign Hill
properties.hook: A hillside spelling out "SOUTH SAN FRANCISCO THE INDUSTRIAL CITY" in giant concrete letters, with a trail that climbs up through them.

properties.name: The Skunk Train
properties.hook: A logging railroad from 1885 still climbs off the Mendocino coast into the redwoods, and you can ride it in a rail motorcar or pedal it yourself on an electric railbike.

properties.name: Slab City
properties.hook: On the concrete slabs of a torn-down WWII base by the Salton Sea, a few hundred people live off the grid year-round in what they call the last free place in America.

properties.name: SLAC (the two-mile accelerator)
properties.hook: A particle accelerator two miles long running under Interstate 280, once the longest building in the world, where they smashed atoms into Nobel Prizes.

properties.name: Solstice Canyon
properties.hook: An easy Malibu canyon walk ends at the ruins of a modernist ranch house built into a waterfall, burned out and left to the creek.

properties.name: Spruce Street Suspension Bridge
properties.hook: A wooden footbridge from 1912 hangs on steel cables over a wooded canyon in the middle of San Diego, and it sways when you walk across it.

properties.name: SS Palo Alto (the Cement Ship)
properties.hook: A concrete ocean tanker — built when WWI steel ran short — has been beached at the end of the Seacliff pier since 1930, spending its century as a dance-hall, a fishing spot, and now a crumbling seabird reef.

properties.name: Star of India
properties.hook: The oldest sailing ship in the world that still actually sails is tied up on the San Diego waterfront, and once a year they take her out.

properties.name: Stinson Beach
properties.hook: The Bay Area's classic beach day: a long arc of sand under Mount Tam, water warm enough to actually swim on a good day, and a trailhead for a legendary hike.

properties.name: Stonewall Peak & Cuyamaca
properties.hook: An hour east of San Diego the land climbs into a pine-and-oak mountain range, and a chain-assisted scramble tops out on a granite dome over the whole park.

properties.name: Sue-meg State Park
properties.hook: The first California park to get its name back: Sue-meg, as the Yurok have always called this headland, with a living village site and a beach where people hunt agates.

properties.name: Sugarloaf Ridge & the Ferguson Observatory
properties.hook: A state park in the Sonoma hills holds the largest observatory in the West built purely for the public, under some of the darkest skies near the Bay.

properties.name: Sundial Bridge
properties.hook: A white spar leans over the Sacramento River in Redding, holding up a glass-decked footbridge that is also a working sundial, one of the largest in the world.

properties.name: Sunken City
properties.hook: A cliffside neighborhood in San Pedro slid into the ocean starting in 1929, and the broken streets and foundations are still out there on the bluff, tilted and graffitied, behind a fence people keep breaking.

properties.name: Sunny Jim's Sea Cave
properties.hook: The only sea cave in California you can reach on foot is at the bottom of a hand-dug tunnel that starts inside a La Jolla gift shop.

properties.name: Sunnylands (the Annenberg Estate)
properties.hook: Behind a pink wall in Rancho Mirage sits the mid-century estate where presidents and kings came to talk, its gardens now free and open to anyone.

properties.name: Sunset Cliffs
properties.hook: A mile and a half of sandstone bluffs on the edge of Point Loma, cut into arches and sea caves, and about the best place in San Diego to watch the sun go down.

properties.name: Sutro Heights Park
properties.hook: A silver baron built a clifftop estate above the Cliff House, filled its gardens with two hundred statues, and opened it to the public for a dime; the mansion is gone but the parapet and the lions remain.

properties.name: Sutter Buttes
properties.hook: A ring of eroded volcanic domes rises 2,000 feet straight out of the flat Sacramento Valley — often called the world's smallest mountain range, a whole extinct volcano you can drive around in twenty minutes but almost never enter.

properties.name: Sutter's Fort
properties.hook: The adobe fort a Swiss immigrant built in 1839 became the seed the whole city of Sacramento grew around, and it's still standing in the middle of it.

properties.name: Swami's & the Self-Realization Fellowship
properties.hook: A world-famous surf break named itself after the swami whose ashram sits on the bluff above it, and the ashram's meditation garden is open, quiet, and free.

properties.name: Sweeney Ridge & the Bay Discovery Site
properties.hook: The ridge where a lost Spanish expedition first laid eyes on San Francisco Bay, now a windswept hike with the whole thing at your feet.

properties.name: Switzer Falls
properties.hook: A shaded creek canyon in the mountains above Pasadena drops through granite pools to a fifty-foot waterfall, past the ghost of a Victorian resort with a chapel over the falls.

properties.name: Taft Point & Sentinel Dome
properties.hook: One trailhead on Glacier Point Road serves both of Yosemite's best cheap thrills: a guardrail-free ledge hanging 3,000 feet over the Valley, and a bald dome with a 360° view that embarrasses Glacier Point itself.

properties.name: Tahoe East Shore Coves
properties.hook: The Nevada side's granite-boulder coves are the Caribbean of the Sierra — and between the famous beach and the nude one runs a string of pocket coves most visitors never find.

properties.name: Tahquitz Canyon
properties.hook: A sixty-foot waterfall runs down a canyon on Cahuilla land minutes from downtown Palm Springs, in a place their oldest story calls home to a banished shaman.

properties.name: Tassajara Zen Mountain Center
properties.hook: A Zen monastery and clothing-optional hot springs at the end of fourteen miles of dirt road, open to outsiders four months a year.

properties.name: Tennessee Valley & Tennessee Cove
properties.hook: A flat valley walk in the Marin Headlands that ends at a black-sand cove named for a gold-rush steamship that wrecked there in 1853 — and whose engine still surfaces at extreme low tides.

properties.name: The Bike Hut
properties.hook: An unlocked garage on a farm road, stocked with snacks, coffee, and spare tubes, that runs entirely on the honor system for whoever rides by.

properties.name: The Broad
properties.hook: A major contemporary-art museum downtown, wrapped in a honeycomb shell, is free to walk into, and it holds one of Yayoi Kusama's infinity mirror rooms.

properties.name: The Dish
properties.hook: A giant Cold War radio telescope on a grassy hill, ringed by a paved loop that half of Palo Alto treats as its living room.

properties.name: The Huntington
properties.hook: A railroad fortune bought a Gutenberg Bible, Gainsborough's Blue Boy, and a hundred and twenty acres of gardens, and left all of it open to the public in San Marino.

properties.name: The Wedge
properties.hook: At the end of the Balboa Peninsula, waves fold back on themselves against a jetty and stack into freak peaks up to thirty feet that break in a few feet of water, and it exists by accident.

properties.name: Thousand Island Lake
properties.hook: An alpine lake scattered with rocky islets sits under Banner Peak, and it's the reflection Ansel Adams made famous.

properties.name: Thousand Palms Oasis
properties.hook: The San Andreas Fault pushes groundwater to the surface in the middle of the desert here, and native fan palms have grown up around it into shady green oases you cross on a boardwalk.

properties.name: Tilden Park Steam Trains
properties.hook: Two separate miniature railways sit side by side in the Berkeley hills — a coal-fired steam line that hauls you through actual redwoods, and next door a hobbyist club running their own home-built locomotives.

properties.name: 16th Avenue Tiled Steps
properties.hook: A staircase in the far avenues that a whole neighborhood turned into a single sea-to-sky mosaic, and almost no visitor makes it out here.

properties.name: Tomales Bay Bioluminescence Paddle
properties.hook: On moonless summer and fall nights, Tomales Bay glows — every paddle stroke ignites blue-green fire, fish trail comet streaks, and your wake burns behind you.

properties.name: Tomales Bay Oysters
properties.hook: Shuck your own oysters at a picnic table on the edge of a quiet bay, or paddle it on a dark night when the water glows blue.

properties.name: Tomales Point & the Tule Elk Reserve
properties.hook: Elk on the tip of a peninsula, ocean on one side and Tomales Bay on the other, and usually no one else on the trail.

properties.name: Torrey Pines Gliderport
properties.hook: On the cliffs above Black's Beach, people have been throwing themselves off the edge under a wing since 1936, and you can stand at the launch and watch, or go up yourself.

properties.name: Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve
properties.hook: The rarest pine in North America grows wild in only two places on Earth, and one of them is a set of eroded sea cliffs just north of La Jolla.

properties.name: The Tourist Club (Die Naturfreunde)
properties.hook: A Bavarian-style lodge hidden on the side of Mount Tam that pours cold beer on a sunny deck, but only for hikers, and only on certain Sundays.

properties.name: Trinidad
properties.hook: A fishing village of fewer than four hundred people sits over a rock-studded harbor on the far north coast, with ten beaches within a few minutes of its one main street.

properties.name: Tuolumne Meadows & the Tioga High Country
properties.hook: An hour past the Valley crowds, Tioga Road climbs into a different park entirely: subalpine meadows at 8,600 feet, glacier-polished domes, and lakes you can actually hear.

properties.name: Upper Newport Bay
properties.hook: Tucked behind the yacht harbors of Newport Beach is one of the last big estuaries in Southern California, loud with birds.

properties.name: Urban Light
properties.hook: Two hundred and two antique street lamps stand in a grid outside the art museum on Wilshire, lit every night, free to walk into whenever you want.

properties.name: Uvas Canyon
properties.hook: A pocket canyon behind Morgan Hill stacks half a dozen waterfalls along a single one-mile loop — the South Bay's rain-day secret, gated by a reservation system that keeps it sane.

properties.name: Vasquez Rocks
properties.hook: The tilted sandstone slabs where Captain Kirk fought the Gorn stand an hour north of LA, shoved up on end by the San Andreas Fault.

properties.name: The Velaslavasay Panorama
properties.hook: In a 1910 theater in West Adams, an artist revived a dead nineteenth-century art form: a painting that wraps all the way around you until you can't tell where the room ends.

properties.name: The Venice Canals
properties.hook: A few blocks off the chaos of the Venice boardwalk is a silent neighborhood of real canals, with footbridges and ducks and no cars, that almost got paved over for good.

properties.name: Vicente Flat & Cone Peak
properties.hook: A backpacking route from the edge of the highway to the tallest coastal mountain in the lower 48, and a redwood camp one local calls the best on the planet.

properties.name: Victoria Beach Pirate Tower
properties.hook: A sixty-foot stone tower shaped like a castle turret stands on a Laguna Beach cove, and the tide has to be out for you to reach it.

properties.name: Villa Montalvo
properties.hook: A senator built an Italian Mediterranean villa into the Saratoga foothills in 1912, filled it with carved Granada doors and hand-worked woodwork, and left it to California — where it became one of the country's oldest artist residencies, ringed by free hiking trails.

properties.name: Waterdog Lake
properties.hook: A hidden reservoir at the top of a Belmont street, a genuine little lake tucked in the hills with trails all around it.

properties.name: The Watts Towers
properties.hook: An Italian immigrant tile-setter spent thirty-three years building seventeen steel towers in his Watts backyard out of broken glass and pottery, the tallest a hundred feet, then handed over the deed and walked away.

properties.name: The Wave Organ
properties.hook: A musical instrument you play by waiting for the tide, built out of a torn-down cemetery at the tip of a Marina jetty.

properties.name: Weaverville Joss House
properties.hook: In a gold-rush town four hours north, the oldest continuously used Chinese temple in California still burns incense at an altar carried over from China in the 1870s.

properties.name: The Wende Museum
properties.hook: A museum in a Cold War National Guard armory in Culver City holds the world's largest collection of things from East Germany, and the longest stretch of the Berlin Wall outside Berlin sits out front.

properties.name: Whitewater Preserve
properties.hook: A cold river runs year-round through a canyon on the desert's edge, and around it a former trout farm has become one of the loveliest oases in Southern California.

properties.name: Wigwam Motel (Village
properties.hook: You can still sleep in a concrete teepee on Route 66 in Rialto, one of the last three survivors of a lost roadside chain and the model for a Pixar movie.

properties.name: Wilder Ranch
properties.hook: A perfectly preserved 1890s dairy ranch sits at the foot of a staircase of fault-lifted coastal terraces just north of Santa Cruz — 7,000 acres where you walk or ride out onto sea-cliff prairie and, on the way, tour a Victorian farmstead still running its water-powered machine shop.

properties.name: Winchester Mystery House
properties.hook: A rifle heiress spent 38 years building a mansion with staircases into ceilings and doors onto two-story drops — and stopped only when she died.

properties.name: The Wind Harp
properties.hook: A 92-foot steel harp on a hilltop by the bay, played by nothing but the wind.

properties.name: Windansea Beach
properties.hook: A rocky La Jolla surf beach guarded by a little palm-frond shack from 1946 that's now an official historical landmark, and by a surf culture famous enough that Tom Wolfe wrote a book about it.

properties.name: Windy Hill
properties.hook: Two bald grassy summits on the Skyline ridge with a full-circle view: bay and valley on one side, ridges rolling to the ocean on the other.

properties.name: The Wisdom Tree
properties.hook: One pine tree on a Hollywood ridgeline survived the fire that took everything around it in 2007, and people hike up to leave their thoughts in a box beneath it.

properties.name: Wolf House (Jack London Park)
properties.hook: The stone ruins of the dream mansion Jack London built and never got to live in, burned to the ground days before he moved in.

properties.name: The Woodside Store
properties.hook: At the bottom of Kings Mountain Road sits an 1854 general store that was also the post office, the saloon, the dentist, and the library, kept just about as it was.

properties.name: Wunderlich Park
properties.hook: A redwood-and-oak forest above Woodside, threaded with old horse-estate trails, that stays quiet while the famous parks fill up.

properties.name: Horsetail Fall "Firefall"
properties.hook: For about two weeks each February, if the water is flowing and the sky is clear, the setting sun turns a thin waterfall on El Capitan into what looks exactly like molten fire pouring down the granite.

properties.name: Yosemite Valley (done right)
properties.hook: The most famous seven square miles of granite on Earth — and it still beats the hype, if you refuse to experience it as a drive-through.

properties.name: Belovezhskaya Pushcha
properties.hook: The Belarusian half of Europe's last great primeval lowland forest, home to wild European bison, and the quiet lodge in the woods where leaders signed the agreement that dissolved the Soviet Union.

properties.name: Brest Fortress
properties.hook: The ruined 19th-century fortress on the Polish border where a Soviet garrison held out against the German invasion in 1941, now one of the most monumental and emotionally charged war memorials of the former USSR.

properties.name: Minsk
properties.hook: A capital almost entirely destroyed in the Second World War and rebuilt from scratch as a showcase of Stalinist city planning, with a vast central avenue of monumental façades that reads like a Soviet stage set.

properties.name: Bruges
properties.hook: A medieval Flemish trading city that grew rich, then fell asleep for centuries when its harbour silted up, and woke to find its canals, guild houses, and belfry perfectly preserved.

properties.name: Brussels
properties.hook: A capital that is easy to underrate, with one of the most ornate market squares in Europe, whole neighbourhoods of Art Nouveau, comic-strip murals on the walls, and a serious appetite for chocolate, beer, and fries.

properties.name: Ghent
properties.hook: Bruges's bigger, grittier neighbour and, for many, the better one: a medieval Flemish city just as handsome but full of students and real life, home to the extraordinary Van Eyck altarpiece.

properties.name: Ganvié
properties.hook: A whole town of tens of thousands built on stilts over a lagoon, where the streets are water, everyone travels by pirogue, and there is even a floating market, founded as a refuge that a spiritual taboo made untouchable.

properties.name: Ouidah
properties.hook: The spiritual home of voodoo, where a temple full of sacred pythons sits near a haunting slave route that ends at a "door of no return" on the beach, a town where a living African religion and a terrible history meet.

properties.name: Pendjari National Park
properties.hook: One of the last strongholds of wildlife in West Africa, a remote savanna park in Benin's far north that shelters some of the region's last lions, elephants, and hippos, far from the famous safari lands of the east and south.

properties.name: The Bumthang Valleys
properties.hook: The spiritual heartland of Bhutan, a group of high central valleys thick with the country's oldest and holiest temples, sacred lakes, and the smell of woodsmoke and buckwheat, far up the mountain road from the tourist west.

properties.name: Thimphu & Punakha
properties.hook: The world's only capital with no traffic lights, gateway to a Buddhist kingdom that measures itself by Gross National Happiness, and to Punakha, whose great fortress-monastery sits where two rivers meet like a stone ship.

properties.name: The Tiger's Nest (Paro Taktsang)
properties.hook: A whitewashed monastery clinging to a sheer cliff face nine hundred metres above the valley floor, reached by a steep pilgrim path, and the image the whole country is known by.

properties.name: Blagaj Tekija
properties.hook: A white Sufi dervish house built right against the foot of a cliff, where a river bursts full-grown out of a cave in the rock beside it.

properties.name: Mostar & the Stari Most
properties.hook: A graceful Ottoman stone bridge arching high over a green river, blown up during the war and rebuilt stone by stone, where young men still dive from the parapet into the cold water below.

properties.name: Sarajevo
properties.hook: A city in a steep valley where a single street carries you from Ottoman bazaar to Austro-Hungarian boulevard in a few paces, and where the scars of a modern siege are still legible if you look.

properties.name: Chobe National Park
properties.hook: A park famous for its enormous elephant herds, where the best seat is a boat on the river at sunset as hundreds of elephants, buffalo, and hippos come down to drink and cross the water.

properties.name: The Kalahari & Makgadikgadi Pans
properties.hook: A landscape of almost unimaginable emptiness: vast white salt pans, the bed of a long-vanished super-lake, stretching flat to the curved horizon, and the red Kalahari sands where meerkats stand sentinel and desert lions roam.

properties.name: The Okavango Delta
properties.hook: A river that never reaches the sea, spilling instead into the Kalahari to form a vast maze of channels, islands, and flooded plains, where you glide silently past elephants and hippos in a dugout canoe.

properties.name: The Amazon & Manaus
properties.hook: The gateway to the greatest rainforest and river on Earth, an improbable jungle city with a grand opera house left from the rubber boom, where two mighty rivers run side by side without mixing before merging into the Amazon proper.

properties.name: Fernando de Noronha
properties.hook: A remote volcanic archipelago in the Atlantic, often crowned Brazil's most beautiful island, where jagged peaks rise from turquoise water, spinner dolphins gather in their hundreds, and one bay regularly tops lists of the best beaches on Earth.

properties.name: Iguaçu Falls
properties.hook: A wall of water nearly three kilometres wide where hundreds of waterfalls thunder over the jungle in clouds of spray, so overwhelming that a visiting first lady reportedly gasped "poor Niagara."

properties.name: Lençóis Maranhenses
properties.hook: A vast field of pristine white sand dunes that looks like a desert but isn't, because after the rains, thousands of crystal-clear turquoise and green lagoons appear in the valleys between the dunes, and you can swim in them surrounded by sand.

properties.name: The Pantanal
properties.hook: The world's largest tropical wetland, a vast seasonally-flooded plain teeming with more visible wildlife than the Amazon, and by far the best place on Earth to see a wild jaguar.

properties.name: Rio de Janeiro
properties.hook: A city wedged spectacularly between jungle mountains and the sea, where a giant Christ spreads his arms over the bay, cable cars rise to a sugarloaf-shaped peak, and the beaches, samba, and street life spill into one of the world's most beautiful and exuberant urban settings.

properties.name: Salvador & Bahia
properties.hook: The first capital of colonial Brazil and the heart of its Afro-Brazilian soul, a city of gilded churches and pastel colonial mansions ringing with drums, where capoeira, candomblé, and some of the country's most vibrant music and food were born.

properties.name: The Buzludzha Monument
properties.hook: A colossal concrete flying saucer marooned on a Balkan mountaintop, built by the Communist Party in 1981 and left to rot when the regime fell, its mosaics now peeling in the wind.

properties.name: Plovdiv
properties.hook: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Europe, where a Roman theatre still stages performances, painted merchant mansions climb cobbled hills, and a former craftsmen's quarter has turned into an arts district.

properties.name: Rila Monastery
properties.hook: Bulgaria's grandest monastery, set deep in forested mountains, its courtyard ringed by tiers of black-and-white striped arches and blazing with frescoes under a painted porch.

properties.name: Ouagadougou & FESPACO
properties.hook: A dusty, friendly Sahel capital with an outsized cultural life, home to the largest film festival in Africa, which every two years turns the city into the beating heart of African cinema.

properties.name: The Sindou Peaks & Banfora
properties.hook: A wind-carved ridge of narrow stone spires and blades rising from the savanna, near a green corner of Burkina Faso with waterfalls and a lake where hippos wallow among the reeds.

properties.name: Angkor
properties.hook: The temples of a vanished empire spread across the jungle near Siem Reap: the largest religious monument on earth, a bridge of stone gods, faces carved into towers, and a ruined temple strangled by the roots of giant trees.

properties.name: Kampot & the Coast
properties.hook: A sleepy river town of faded colonial shophouses backed by a misty mountain, famous for a pepper prized in the world's best kitchens, with quiet coast and islands close by.

properties.name: Phnom Penh
properties.hook: Cambodia's riverfront capital, where a golden royal palace and a lively market sit a short distance from the prison and the killing field that memorialise one of the 20th century's worst genocides.

properties.name: Mount Cameroon & the Coast
properties.hook: The highest peak in West and Central Africa, an active volcano that rises straight from the Atlantic coast, its slopes climbing from black-sand beaches through rainforest to a bare, ashy summit.

properties.name: Waza National Park
properties.hook: The best-known wildlife park in Cameroon, a stretch of Sahel savanna in the far north where elephants, giraffes, lions, and vast flocks of birds gather, a classic dry-country safari far from the forested south.

properties.name: The Ennedi Massif
properties.hook: A vast sandstone labyrinth in the middle of the Sahara, where erosion has carved soaring natural arches, towers, and canyons, ancient rock art covers the walls, and crocodiles improbably survive in a hidden desert pool.

properties.name: Zakouma National Park
properties.hook: A conservation comeback story in the heart of Africa, a remote savanna park where elephants, once slaughtered in their thousands by poachers, now thrive again alongside vast herds and one of the continent's great bird spectacles.

properties.name: The Atacama Desert
properties.hook: The driest desert on Earth, where some weather stations have never recorded rain, a high, otherworldly landscape of salt flats, steaming geysers, flamingo lagoons, and skies so clear that the world's great telescopes gather here.

properties.name: Santiago & Valparaíso
properties.hook: Chile's contrasting central duo: a modern, orderly capital pressed against a wall of snow-capped Andes, and, over the coastal hills, a gloriously chaotic port city of clattering funiculars, tumbling painted houses, and street art.

properties.name: Torres del Paine
properties.hook: Patagonia's crown jewel: three sheer granite towers soaring above a turquoise glacial lake, at the heart of a national park of glaciers, emerald valleys, and roaming guanacos that draws trekkers from around the world.

properties.name: Beijing
properties.hook: The imperial capital, laid out on a grand ceremonial axis, where you cross the enormous walled Forbidden City the emperors ruled from, then dive into the grey courtyard-house lanes that still survive between the towers.

properties.name: Chengdu & the Pandas
properties.hook: The relaxed capital of Sichuan, famous for teahouses, mouth-numbing hotpot, and the breeding base on its edge where you can watch giant pandas up close as they eat, sleep, and tumble out of trees.

properties.name: The Great Wall
properties.hook: Not one wall but many, built and rebuilt over two thousand years and running for thousands of kilometres across mountains and desert, best met on a steep, half-wild section with a watchtower and no crowd.

properties.name: Guilin & Yangshuo
properties.hook: The landscape from the back of the twenty-yuan note come to life: hundreds of steep, rounded limestone peaks rising like green teeth out of paddy fields and a slow jade-green river winding among them.

properties.name: Lhasa & the Potala
properties.hook: The historic heart of Tibetan Buddhism, high on the plateau at 3,650 metres, where the vast white-and-red Potala Palace rises over the city and pilgrims still shuffle and prostrate their way around the old temple at its centre.

properties.name: Shanghai
properties.hook: China's showcase megacity, where a curved waterfront of 1920s European banks stares across the river at a wall of futuristic towers, and leafy old French Concession lanes hide behind the glass.

properties.name: The Terracotta Army, Xi'an
properties.hook: An entire army of life-size clay soldiers, thousands of them, each with a different face, buried in battle formation to guard a first emperor's tomb and forgotten for two thousand years until a farmer digging a well hit one.

properties.name: Brazzaville
properties.hook: A surprisingly relaxed capital on the great Congo River, gazing across the water at its huge, frenetic twin Kinshasa, famous for rumba, riverside calm, and the flamboyant, impeccably-dressed dandies known as sapeurs.

properties.name: Odzala-Kokoua National Park
properties.hook: Deep in the Congo Basin, the second-largest rainforest on Earth, a park where you track western lowland gorillas through the jungle and watch forest elephants gather in natural clearings, a genuine expedition into the green heart of Africa.

properties.name: Dubrovnik
properties.hook: A walled marble city jutting into the Adriatic, once an independent merchant republic that outmanoeuvred bigger powers for centuries, now so popular that cruise-ship days can make the lanes impassable.

properties.name: Istria
properties.hook: Croatia's northern peninsula, quietly Italian in feel, with a Venetian fishing town stacked on a headland, a nearly complete Roman amphitheatre at Pula, and inland hills full of truffles and medieval villages.

properties.name: Plitvice Lakes
properties.hook: Sixteen lakes stacked in a wooded canyon, each spilling into the next over natural stone dams, the water an unreal turquoise, all crossed on low wooden boardwalks that run right at the surface.

properties.name: Karlovy Vary
properties.hook: A belle-époque spa town strung along a wooded river valley, where the ritual is to stroll grand colonnades sipping warm mineral water from a spouted cup, as Europe's aristocracy did for centuries.

properties.name: Olomouc
properties.hook: A handsome Moravian university town that feels like Prague without the crowds, with a giant Baroque plague column big enough to have a chapel inside it and a cheese famous for its smell.

properties.name: Prague
properties.hook: A Gothic and Baroque city that came through the twentieth century almost unscathed, stacked along a river with a statue-lined stone bridge, a hilltop castle, and a medieval clock that draws a crowd on the hour.

properties.name: Copenhagen
properties.hook: A flat, bike-mad harbour capital where you can swim in the middle of the city, eat some of the world's most celebrated cooking, and feel out what the Danes mean by hygge.

properties.name: Møns Klint
properties.hook: Six kilometres of brilliant white chalk cliffs dropping into the turquoise Baltic on a quiet island, backed by old beech forest and some of the darkest night skies in Denmark.

properties.name: Skagen & Grenen
properties.hook: The northern tip of Denmark, where two seas crash into each other along a sandbar you can walk to and stand with a foot in each, under a clear light that drew a whole colony of painters.

properties.name: The Congo River & Kinshasa
properties.hook: One of the world's great rivers and one of Africa's largest, most electric cities face each other here: the mighty, deep-flowing Congo, and Kinshasa, a roaring metropolis that gave the world Congolese rumba.

properties.name: Virunga & Nyiragongo
properties.hook: Africa's oldest national park, a place of staggering drama: a volcano cradling one of the world's largest permanent lakes of molten lava, mountain gorillas in the forests, and rangers who have died defending it all.

properties.name: Aswan & Abu Simbel
properties.hook: The gentle, Nubian-flavoured southern end of the Nile, where you sail feluccas between granite islands, and, further south, four colossal seated statues guard temples that were sawn apart and lifted to higher ground to save them from a rising lake.

properties.name: Cairo
properties.hook: A thundering megacity of some twenty million on the Nile, where a thousand years of minarets and medieval streets, one of the world's great troves of pharaonic treasure, and relentless modern chaos all press together.

properties.name: The Pyramids of Giza
properties.hook: The last of the ancient world's seven wonders still standing, tombs of stone raised four and a half thousand years ago on the desert edge of Cairo, so old that they were already ancient when the Romans came to gawp.

properties.name: Luxor
properties.hook: Built over ancient Thebes, the capital of pharaonic Egypt at its height, a place often called the world's greatest open-air museum, where colossal temples line one bank of the Nile and the tombs of kings honeycomb the hills on the other.

properties.name: Mount Sinai & St Catherine's
properties.hook: The mountain where, by tradition, Moses received the Ten Commandments, climbed through the night by pilgrims and travellers to watch the sun rise over a sea of bare peaks, with one of the world's oldest monasteries at its foot.

properties.name: The Red Sea Reefs
properties.hook: Some of the most accessible world-class coral reefs on the planet, where you can step off a Sinai beach straight onto walls of fish and coral in warm, staggeringly clear water.

properties.name: The White Desert
properties.hook: A stretch of the Sahara where the wind has carved white chalk into surreal mushrooms, towers, and animal shapes, an eerie moonscape you camp among under a sky thick with stars.

properties.name: Lahemaa National Park
properties.hook: An easy escape from Tallinn into deep Estonian nature: pine forest, boulder-strewn coast, elegant old manor houses, and raised bogs you cross on boardwalks past pools the colour of tea.

properties.name: Saaremaa
properties.hook: Estonia's largest island, a slow, flat world of junipers, wooden windmills, and small stone churches, with a field of craters where a meteorite slammed into the ground within human memory.

properties.name: Tallinn
properties.hook: One of the best-preserved medieval towns in northern Europe, a walled tangle of merchant houses, spires, and gate towers, run by a country so digital you can become an e-resident from your laptop.

properties.name: The Danakil Depression
properties.hook: One of the hottest and lowest places on earth, an alien landscape of neon-yellow sulphur springs, salt flats worked by camel caravans, and a volcano with a lake of molten lava you can peer into at night.

properties.name: Lalibela
properties.hook: A cluster of medieval churches carved downward out of solid rock, entire buildings hewn from the living stone below ground level, still thronged with white-robed pilgrims of one of the world's oldest Christian traditions.

properties.name: The Simien Mountains
properties.hook: A jagged wall of peaks and plunging escarpments in the Ethiopian highlands, often called the roof of Africa, where troops of shaggy, red-chested gelada, found only here, graze the clifftops in their hundreds.

properties.name: The Finnish Lakeland
properties.hook: The heart of Finland is water: thousands of lakes threaded with forest and islands, where the national ritual is a lakeside cabin, a wood-fired sauna, and a jump into cold water.

properties.name: Finnish Lapland
properties.hook: Finland's Arctic north, a land of snow forests and reindeer where winter brings the northern lights and a commercialised but sincere claim to be the home of Santa Claus.

properties.name: Helsinki & Suomenlinna
properties.hook: A calm, sea-facing capital of clean design and public saunas, with a short ferry out to Suomenlinna, an 18th-century fortress spread across a cluster of islands guarding the harbour.

properties.name: Étretat
properties.hook: White chalk cliffs on the Normandy coast that the sea has cut into natural arches and a lone needle of rock, the seascape Monet painted again and again.

properties.name: Lascaux & the Vézère Caves
properties.hook: The Dordogne valley where Ice Age people painted galleries of bulls, horses, and deer seventeen thousand years ago, so vivid the discovery reset what we thought "primitive" meant.

properties.name: The Loire Châteaux
properties.hook: A river valley packed with the pleasure-palaces French kings and nobles built when a castle stopped being a fortress and became a statement, including one with a double-helix staircase said to owe something to Leonardo.

properties.name: Chamonix & the Mer de Glace
properties.hook: France's largest glacier, reached by a little red rack railway, with the ice cave re-carved every summer because the glacier keeps dropping out from under it.

properties.name: Mont-Saint-Michel
properties.hook: A medieval abbey stacked on a rock in a bay with some of the fastest tides in Europe, cut off from land twice a day and floated on the water at the highest ones.

properties.name: The Verdon Gorge
properties.hook: A limestone canyon up to seven hundred metres deep in the hills of Provence, with a river at the bottom the colour of antifreeze.

properties.name: Ivindo & the Rainforest Interior
properties.hook: The green heart of Gabon: a vast tract of pristine equatorial rainforest hiding thundering waterfalls and a legendary forest clearing where elephants, gorillas, and buffalo gather in the open, deep in one of the wildest places on Earth.

properties.name: Loango National Park
properties.hook: Where the rainforest meets the Atlantic in the most surreal way: elephants and buffalo strolling on the beach, hippos surfing in the ocean waves, and gorillas in the forest behind, on a wild, empty coast.

properties.name: Kakheti Wine Country
properties.hook: The heartland of Georgian wine, where people have been making it in buried clay vessels for some eight thousand years, among the oldest winemaking traditions known anywhere, in a landscape of vineyards, hill towns, and old monasteries under the Caucasus.

properties.name: Svaneti
properties.hook: A remote region high in the Caucasus where medieval stone defensive towers still stand in every village beneath snow peaks, and Ushguli, among the highest permanently inhabited settlements in Europe, sits under Georgia's tallest mountain.

properties.name: Tbilisi
properties.hook: A crossroads capital in the Caucasus where wooden-balconied houses lean over cobbled lanes, a fortress looks down from the hill, and you can lower yourself into a domed bathhouse fed by natural hot sulphur springs.

properties.name: The Bastei
properties.hook: A stone bridge from 1851 threading between free-standing sandstone pinnacles nearly two hundred metres above the Elbe, in a forest of rock towers that looks invented for a fairy tale.

properties.name: Berlin
properties.hook: A capital that spent the twentieth century at the centre of its worst events and now confronts that history in the open, from a wall that split the city to a field of grey slabs for the murdered of Europe.

properties.name: The Black Forest
properties.hook: The dark, densely wooded hills of Germany's southwest corner, home of the cuckoo clock, the gateau, and a landscape of fir, waterfalls, and spa towns that fed the country's fairy tales.

properties.name: Hamburg
properties.hook: Germany's great port, a brick city of canals and warehouses where an old Hanseatic trading town meets a wave-shaped glass concert hall on top of a former cocoa store.

properties.name: The Middle Rhine Valley
properties.hook: The stretch of the Rhine where the river carves a steep gorge past a castle roughly every few kilometres, vineyards climbing the slate slopes, and a rock where a siren was said to wreck boatmen.

properties.name: Neuschwanstein Castle
properties.hook: The pale turreted castle that half the world pictures when it hears "fairy-tale castle," built by a reclusive Bavarian king who barely lived in it and died mysteriously before it was finished.

properties.name: Cape Coast & Elmina Castles
properties.hook: Whitewashed forts on a palm-fringed shore whose beauty masks a terrible history: these were holding prisons of the transatlantic slave trade, where captured Africans were chained in dungeons before passing through the "door of no return."

properties.name: Kakum National Park
properties.hook: A rare surviving tract of West African rainforest where a series of rope-and-plank bridges are strung between the treetops, letting you walk high above the forest floor among the birds and the canopy.

properties.name: Mole National Park
properties.hook: Ghana's largest wildlife refuge, a stretch of northern savanna where you can walk on foot with a ranger and, from the lodge's clifftop pool, watch elephants come to drink at the waterhole below.

properties.name: The Acropolis & Athens
properties.hook: The rock at the centre of Athens, crowned by the Parthenon, the building that has defined what "classical" means for two and a half thousand years, still standing over a chaotic modern city.

properties.name: Delphi
properties.hook: The ancient world's most important oracle, set on a terraced mountainside the Greeks believed was the centre of the earth, where kings and cities came to hear the future in riddles.

properties.name: Meteora
properties.hook: Orthodox monasteries built on top of sheer sandstone pillars, reached for centuries only by rope ladders and nets the monks could pull up behind them.

properties.name: Monemvasia
properties.hook: A whole Byzantine town hidden on the seaward face of a giant rock, invisible from the mainland, reached by a single causeway whose name means "one entrance."

properties.name: Naxos
properties.hook: The biggest and greenest of the Cyclades, with mountain villages, long empty beaches, and a giant marble doorway standing alone on an islet, framing the sunset and leading nowhere.

properties.name: Zagori & the Vikos Gorge
properties.hook: A cluster of grey-stone mountain villages in the remote northwest, linked by old packhorse trails and single-arch bridges, above a limestone gorge reckoned among the deepest in the world for its width.

properties.name: The Fouta Djallon
properties.hook: A cool, green highland plateau of sandstone cliffs, deep valleys, and waterfalls, so full of rivers that it feeds three of West Africa's greatest, earning it the name "water tower of West Africa."

properties.name: The Nimba Mountains
properties.hook: A green, forested mountain range straddling three countries, so rich in creatures found nowhere else, including a toad that gives birth to live young and tool-using chimpanzees, that it is protected as one of West Africa's most precious ecological treasures.

properties.name: Budapest
properties.hook: A grand imperial capital split by the Danube into hilly Buda and flat Pest, sitting on hot springs that feed sprawling thermal bath palaces where the city goes to soak and play chess in the steam.

properties.name: Lake Balaton
properties.hook: The largest lake in Central Europe, so central to a landlocked country that Hungarians call it their sea, warm and shallow enough to wade far out, ringed by vineyards and an old abbey on a volcanic tongue of land.

properties.name: Tokaj Wine Country
properties.hook: The hills where one of the world's original great sweet wines is made, from grapes deliberately left to shrivel with noble rot, once served at royal courts and dubbed the wine of kings.

properties.name: Jökulsárlón (the glacier lagoon)
properties.hook: Icebergs the size of houses break off a glacier and drift across a lagoon that didn't exist a century ago, then wash up as polished blocks on a black-sand beach.

properties.name: Landmannalaugar (the rainbow mountains)
properties.hook: Mountains streaked red, green, and blue rise over a valley where a hot river runs warm enough to bathe in, and for most of the year no road can reach it.

properties.name: Hampi
properties.hook: The ruins of one of the greatest cities of the medieval world, temples and palaces scattered for miles across a surreal landscape of giant balancing boulders, sacked and abandoned five centuries ago and never rebuilt.

properties.name: Jaipur & Rajasthan
properties.hook: The capital of Rajasthan, a planned 18th-century city painted terracotta pink, backed by hilltop forts and palaces, and the gateway to India's most romantic region of desert kingdoms.

properties.name: The Kerala Backwaters
properties.hook: A green maze of palm-lined canals, lakes, and rice paddies along the southwest coast, where villages live on the water and you drift through it all on a converted rice barge.

properties.name: The Khajuraho Temples
properties.hook: A group of thousand-year-old Hindu and Jain temples in the middle of nowhere, their walls covered in some of the finest stone carving in India, including the frankly erotic sculptures they are famous for.

properties.name: Rishikesh & the Himalayan Foothills
properties.hook: Where the Ganges leaves the Himalaya and runs clear and cold through a town of ashrams, yoga schools, and swaying footbridges, the self-styled yoga capital of the world and the spot where the Beatles once came to meditate.

properties.name: The Taj Mahal, Agra
properties.hook: A white marble tomb a grieving emperor built for his wife, so perfectly proportioned that the most photographed building on earth still stops people cold when they walk through the gate and see it for real.

properties.name: Varanasi
properties.hook: One of the oldest continuously lived-in cities on earth and the holiest in Hinduism, where life and death happen openly on the steps down to the Ganges, and Hindus come to die because doing so here is said to end the cycle of rebirth.

properties.name: Bali
properties.hook: A single Hindu island in the world's largest Muslim country, where daily offerings sit on every doorstep, rice terraces stair-step down the hills, and temples cling to sea cliffs, all of it now wrestling with its own popularity.

properties.name: Borobudur
properties.hook: The largest Buddhist temple in the world, a mountain of carved stone built around the year 800, lost under volcanic ash and jungle for centuries, its terraces climbing toward a crown of bell-shaped stupas above the Javanese plain.

properties.name: Komodo National Park
properties.hook: A cluster of dry, rugged islands that are the only home of the Komodo dragon, the largest lizard on earth, set in some of the richest diving waters in the world, with a beach the colour of rose.

properties.name: Lake Toba
properties.hook: An enormous lake filling the crater of one of the largest volcanic eruptions in the history of the planet, with an island nearly the size of Singapore sitting in the middle of it, home to the Batak people.

properties.name: Mount Bromo & Ijen
properties.hook: A smoking volcano cone standing in a vast sea of ash, ringed by an ancient caldera, and a nearby crater where miners carry sulphur out past electric-blue flames that only show in the dark.

properties.name: Tana Toraja
properties.hook: A green highland in Sulawesi where death is the centre of life: elaborate multi-day funerals, the dead kept at home for months or years before burial, and graves carved into cliffs guarded by wooden effigies.

properties.name: Tanjung Puting & the Orangutans
properties.hook: A Borneo national park where you chug up a jungle river on a wooden liveaboard boat to watch wild and rehabilitated orangutans, our red-haired cousins, swing down to forest feeding platforms.

properties.name: Chogha Zanbil
properties.hook: A colossal mud-brick ziggurat rising from the plain of southwestern Iran, built by the little-known Elamite civilisation over three thousand years ago, one of the best-preserved stepped temple-towers left outside Mesopotamia.

properties.name: Isfahan
properties.hook: The jewel of old Persia, built around one of the largest public squares on earth and lined with tiled blue mosques, a palace, and a bazaar, a city so beautiful that a Persian rhyme calls it "half the world."

properties.name: Persepolis
properties.hook: The ceremonial capital of the mighty Persian Empire, a terrace of colossal columns and stairways carved with processions of the empire's subjects bringing tribute, burned by Alexander the Great and left as majestic ruin.

properties.name: Shiraz
properties.hook: The Persian city of poets, roses, and nightingales, where a mosque's stained-glass windows scatter the morning sun into a carpet of colour, and locals still gather at the tomb of a beloved poet to read his verse aloud.

properties.name: Tehran
properties.hook: Iran's sprawling, mountain-backed capital, where the old shahs' mirror-tiled palaces and a dazzling vault of crown jewels sit amid a chaotic modern metropolis of galleries, cafés, and famously heavy traffic.

properties.name: Yazd
properties.hook: A honey-coloured desert city of mud-brick lanes and rooftops bristling with windcatchers, ancient towers that funnel breezes down into the houses, and a fire that Zoroastrians say has burned without pause for over a thousand years.

properties.name: Babylon & Baghdad
properties.hook: The ruins of Babylon, one of the most famous cities of the ancient world, home to the legendary Hanging Gardens and the great blue Ishtar Gate, on the plains south of Baghdad, a heartland of civilisation now hard and risky to reach.

properties.name: Erbil & Iraqi Kurdistan
properties.hook: A great mounded citadel rising over a modern city, claimed as one of the longest continuously inhabited places on earth, at the heart of Iraqi Kurdistan, the country's most stable and welcoming region.

properties.name: Ur & the Mesopotamian Marshes
properties.hook: The great ziggurat of Ur, a stepped brick temple-mountain from one of humanity's first cities, on the edge of the Mesopotamian marshes, a watery world of reed islands where a distinctive culture has lived for five thousand years.

properties.name: The Cliffs of Moher & the Burren
properties.hook: A wall of black cliffs dropping some two hundred metres straight into the Atlantic, next to the Burren, a grey limestone desert where Arctic and Mediterranean flowers grow side by side in the cracks.

properties.name: Connemara
properties.hook: The wild, boggy west of Galway, where the Irish language is still spoken day to day, quartzite mountains rise out of brown moorland, and a Victorian castle sits reflected in a lake below them.

properties.name: Newgrange & the Boyne Tombs
properties.hook: A grass-covered stone mound built around 3200 BC, older than Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids, engineered so that at the winter solstice a beam of sunrise light runs straight down a long passage into the burial chamber.

properties.name: The Park of the Monsters (Bomarzo)
properties.hook: A grieving Renaissance prince spent decades filling the wood below his castle with giant stone monsters: a screaming hell-mouth you can walk into, a war elephant crushing a soldier, a house built deliberately crooked. Then it was forgotten for three hundred years.

properties.name: Civita di Bagnoregio (the dying town)
properties.hook: A medieval town sits alone on a crumbling pinnacle of rock, reachable only by a long footbridge, and every year a little more of it drops into the valley. They call it the town that is dying.

properties.name: The Dolomites
properties.hook: A whole range of pale limestone towers that turn rose and orange at sunset, standing over green meadows instead of the usual grey Alpine scree. Old coral reefs, pushed two miles into the sky.

properties.name: Mount Etna
properties.hook: The most active volcano in Europe, and one you can actually climb toward, walking over black lava that was liquid a few years ago while the summit smokes above you.

properties.name: The Sassi of Matera
properties.hook: A town of houses dug into a limestone ravine, lived in for something like nine thousand years. Seventy years ago the Italian state called it a national disgrace and emptied it by force.

properties.name: Pompeii & Herculaneum
properties.hook: Two Roman towns buried alive by Vesuvius in AD 79 and dug back out, one enormous and famous, the other smaller and far better preserved, with wooden doors, upstairs floors, and carbonised food still in place.

properties.name: Ravenna's Mosaics
properties.hook: An unassuming town in the flatlands that was once the capital of the western Roman Empire, holding some of the best-preserved Byzantine mosaics anywhere, ceilings of gold and deep blue from the sixth century.

properties.name: The Val d'Orcia
properties.hook: The exact Tuscany of the imagination, rolling wheat and clay hills with lone cypress rows leading to farmhouses, wrapped around wine towns that take their reds very seriously.

properties.name: Abidjan & Grand-Bassam
properties.hook: West Africa's slickest, most cosmopolitan city, spread across lagoons and skylines with a buzzing music and food scene, next door to a crumbling former colonial capital of faded mansions on the beach.

properties.name: The Yamoussoukro Basilica
properties.hook: One of the largest churches in the world, a colossal domed basilica modelled on St Peter's in Rome, rising improbably out of a small, half-empty planned city in the middle of West Africa.

properties.name: Hiroshima & Miyajima
properties.hook: The city rebuilt from the world's first atomic bombing, where a skeletal ruined dome is kept exactly as the blast left it, paired with a nearby island whose great vermilion shrine gate appears to float at high tide.

properties.name: The Snow Monkeys of Jigokudani
properties.hook: In the mountains of Nagano, wild monkeys have learned to do what no other wild monkeys on Earth do: sit up to their necks in a hot spring while the snow comes down, and soak.

properties.name: Kanazawa
properties.hook: A wealthy old castle town on the Japan Sea coast that came through history intact, with one of the country's three great gardens, preserved geisha and samurai quarters, and a tradition of gold leaf so refined the locals put it on ice cream.

properties.name: Mount Kōya (Koyasan)
properties.hook: On a mountaintop wrapped in cedar forest sits a thousand-year-old monastic town, and at the far end of its graveyard a monk has been sitting in meditation, his followers believe, for twelve hundred years.

properties.name: Kyoto
properties.hook: Japan's old imperial capital, spared the wartime bombing, holding more than a thousand years of temples, Zen gardens, and geisha districts, and now so popular that the trick is dodging the crowds it draws.

properties.name: Mount Fuji & Hakone
properties.hook: Japan's highest mountain and its great sacred symbol, a near-perfect volcanic cone that has been painted and climbed for centuries, most reliably seen from the hot-spring valleys at its foot in clear winter air.

properties.name: Tokyo
properties.hook: One of the world's largest cities, and one that works: a sprawl of distinct neighbourhoods stitched together by immaculate trains, where a thousand-year-old shrine sits a block from a neon canyon and the food is extraordinary at every price.

properties.name: Yakushima
properties.hook: A near-circular island of mountains and rainforest off Japan's southern tip, drenched in rain and moss, where cedar trees thousands of years old grow in a green half-light that inspired the forest of Princess Mononoke.

properties.name: The Dead Sea
properties.hook: The lowest point on the surface of the earth, a salt lake so dense you cannot sink, where the novelty is bobbing on your back like a cork while reading a newspaper.

properties.name: Jerash
properties.hook: One of the best-preserved Roman provincial cities anywhere, where you walk an intact colonnaded main street rutted by chariot wheels, into a rare oval forum ringed by columns, all in golden stone.

properties.name: Madaba & Mount Nebo
properties.hook: A town whose churches hold the oldest surviving map of the Holy Land, laid out in Byzantine mosaic on a floor, beside the hill where, tradition says, Moses looked out over a promised land he would never enter.

properties.name: Petra
properties.hook: A whole city carved into rose-coloured sandstone cliffs by an ancient desert trading people, entered through a long narrow canyon that opens, without warning, on a temple façade cut straight into the rock.

properties.name: Wadi Rum
properties.hook: A desert of red sand and towering sandstone and granite mountains, so otherworldly it stands in for Mars in the movies, where Lawrence of Arabia rode and Bedouin still run camps under skies thick with stars.

properties.name: Almaty & the Tian Shan
properties.hook: Kazakhstan's green former capital, sitting right under the snow peaks of the Tian Shan, in the region where the apple is thought to have first grown wild.

properties.name: Astana
properties.hook: A brand-new capital conjured out of the bare steppe in a single generation, a showcase of wild futuristic architecture rising from flat grassland, and one of the coldest capital cities in the world.

properties.name: Mangystau
properties.hook: A remote desert region by the Caspian where the earth turns alien: white chalk canyons, valleys of strange eroded pinnacles, mosques carved deep underground, and rock that looks like another planet.

properties.name: Amboseli
properties.hook: The classic image of Africa: herds of great elephants crossing a dusty plain with the snow-capped dome of Kilimanjaro, Africa's highest mountain, floating behind them across the border in Tanzania.

properties.name: Lake Nakuru & the Rift Lakes
properties.hook: A shallow soda lake in the floor of the Great Rift Valley that can turn pink with vast flocks of flamingos, ringed by a park that is one of Kenya's best places to see rhinos.

properties.name: The Maasai Mara
properties.hook: The classic African savanna of the imagination, endless golden grassland dotted with acacias and big cats, and the stage for the great migration, when millions of wildebeest and zebra pour across in a thundering, crocodile-menaced river crossing.

properties.name: Nairobi & Mount Kenya
properties.hook: The only capital city on earth with a full national park on its doorstep, where lions and rhinos roam against a skyline of tower blocks, gateway to the glaciered second-highest mountain in Africa.

properties.name: Samburu & the Northern Frontier
properties.hook: A hot, semi-arid land north of the highlands, drier and wilder than the famous parks, home to a cast of desert-adapted species found almost nowhere else and to the proud, red-ochre Samburu people.

properties.name: The Swahili Coast & Lamu
properties.hook: An ancient trading culture where Africa met Arabia and India across the monsoon-borne Indian Ocean, its oldest living town a car-free maze of coral-stone houses and carved doors, where dhows still sail and donkeys carry the loads.

properties.name: Failaka Island
properties.hook: A low island off Kuwait City layered with the ruins of a Greek settlement founded in the wake of Alexander the Great, and, from a much more recent conflict, a bullet-scarred town abandoned since the Gulf War and never reinhabited.

properties.name: Kuwait City
properties.hook: A wealthy, low-key Gulf capital where the iconic blue-globed water towers stand over a modern waterfront, and a bustling old souq and museums recall a merchant and pearling past, and a recent war.

properties.name: Lake Issyk-Kul & the Tian Shan
properties.hook: One of the world's largest and deepest mountain lakes, a vast blue sheet of slightly salty water that never freezes, ringed by the snow peaks of the Tian Shan and the summer camps of herders.

properties.name: Osh & the Southern Silk Road
properties.hook: One of Central Asia's oldest cities, a Silk Road crossroads in the fertile Ferghana Valley, where a sprawling ancient bazaar spreads beneath a craggy sacred mountain that pilgrims have climbed for millennia.

properties.name: Song-Köl & the Jailoos
properties.hook: A high mountain lake on a treeless plateau at three thousand metres, where Kyrgyz herders bring their animals and yurts each summer, and the night sky is thick with stars far from any light.

properties.name: The 4000 Islands (Si Phan Don)
properties.hook: Where the Mekong braids into a scatter of river islands just before Cambodia, a sleepy, hammock-slung backwater of rice paddies and old French railway relics, with rare river dolphins and thundering falls nearby.

properties.name: Luang Prabang
properties.hook: A serene old royal town on a finger of land between the Mekong and a tributary, full of gilded temples and French-colonial villas, where at dawn lines of barefoot monks collect alms through the mist.

properties.name: The Plain of Jars
properties.hook: Hundreds of huge, ancient stone jars scattered across highland meadows, carved by an unknown people for reasons no one is sure of, in a region that was also one of the most heavily bombed places on earth.

properties.name: The Gauja Valley & Sigulda
properties.hook: A green river valley of sandstone bluffs, forest, and old castles that Latvians call their Switzerland, threaded with legends and reachable by a cable car strung across the gorge.

properties.name: The Latgale Lakes
properties.hook: The quiet, lake-strewn southeast corner of Latvia, poorer and more mixed than the rest, where blue lakes, Orthodox and Catholic churches, and a living folk-pottery tradition sit far off any tourist trail.

properties.name: Riga
properties.hook: The biggest of the Baltic capitals, pairing a Hanseatic old town with entire streets of extravagant Art Nouveau apartment buildings, faces and mythical beasts staring down from the façades.

properties.name: Baalbek
properties.hook: The largest Roman temple complex ever built, where columns the height of a seven-storey building still stand and a near-complete temple rises over foundation stones so colossal no one is entirely sure how they were moved.

properties.name: Beirut
properties.hook: A battered, brilliant Mediterranean capital that has been destroyed and rebuilt more times than almost any city, layering Roman ruins, Ottoman and French quarters, war scars, and a famously defiant appetite for food and nightlife.

properties.name: The Cedars & the Qadisha Valley
properties.hook: A deep holy gorge where hermits and Christian communities carved monasteries into the cliffs, above it a surviving grove of the ancient cedars that once covered these mountains and gave Lebanon its emblem.

properties.name: Monrovia & the Surf Coast
properties.hook: A rough, resilient capital named after an American president, born from the return of freed slaves to Africa, on an Atlantic coast that hides some of West Africa's best and emptiest surf.

properties.name: Sapo National Park
properties.hook: Liberia's largest protected rainforest and the second-largest remaining block of primary forest in West Africa, a dense, dripping wilderness hiding forest elephants, chimpanzees, and the elusive pygmy hippo.

properties.name: The Akakus & the Fezzan
properties.hook: A desert range in Libya's deep southwest where the sand and rock rise into soaring natural arches and pinnacles, their walls covered with prehistoric paintings of a green Sahara that vanished thousands of years ago.

properties.name: Leptis Magna
properties.hook: One of the most spectacular and complete Roman cities anywhere, a vast marble metropolis on the Libyan coast, lavished with grandeur by an emperor born there, and long preserved by the desert sand that buried it.

properties.name: The Curonian Spit
properties.hook: A thread of sand barely a few kilometres wide running a hundred kilometres down the Baltic, with some of the highest drifting dunes in Europe on one side and a calm lagoon on the other.

properties.name: The Hill of Crosses
properties.hook: A low mound near Šiauliai carpeted in hundreds of thousands of crosses, planted over generations as a stubborn act of faith and defiance that Soviet bulldozers could not stop.

properties.name: Vilnius
